abstract:
Quality higher education is a cornerstone of national socio-economic development, with service quality serving as a critical metric for institutional success. This study investigates the persistent challenges facing the Ethiopian higher education sector amidst a period of rapid institutional expansion. Utilizing a qualitative research design, the study integrated data from document analysis, focus group discussions, and interviews. A systematic review was conducted on 89 research papers, supplemented by an extensive evaluation of government policy documents, peer-reviewed articles, doctoral dissertations, and empirical reports addressing expansion and quality standards. 

The findings indicate that despite the implementation of various quality improvement programs and regulatory frameworks, these initiatives have had a negligible impact on the overall quality of the sector. Most Ethiopian higher education institutions continue to struggle with a significant shortage of experienced academic staff and chronic deficiencies in essential infrastructure, including ICT systems, library facilities, laboratory equipment, and adequate classroom space. Furthermore, poor dormitory services remain a major grievance for the student body. The study concludes that the majority of higher education institutions in Ethiopia are failing to deliver satisfactory service quality, potentially undermining student learning outcomes. Based on these systemic gaps, specific policy recommendations are proposed to better align the country’s aggressive expansion strategy with sustainable quality standards.
